title: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) - Unauthorized Read Access to Other Users' Drafts
description: Any authenticated user can access and view the drafts of any other user by navigating to /<target_username>/drafts. The application does not verify that the currently authenticated user is the owner of the requested drafts. This allows a low-privilege user to read confidential draft content belonging to any account, including the admin account.
steps: 1. Register and log in as testuser (or any valid user). 2. Navigate to http://blog:5003/admin/drafts — observe that the admin's private drafts are returned with HTTP 200 and full content. 3. Repeat for any other username (e.g. /user/drafts) to enumerate all users' drafts.

title: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) - Unauthorized Write/Modification of Other Users' Drafts
description: Any authenticated user can modify any draft in the application by submitting a POST request to /edit_draft/<id> with an arbitrary draft ID. No authorization check verifies that the requesting user owns the specified draft. This was confirmed by modifying admin's draft (ID=1) as testuser, changing its title to 'HACKED'. An attacker can corrupt, overwrite, or tamper with any user's draft content.
steps: 1. Log in as testuser. 2. Send POST to http://blog:5003/edit_draft/1 with body title=HACKED&content=modified_by_attacker. 3. Visit http://blog:5003/admin/drafts — confirm the draft title has changed to 'HACKED'. 4. Any integer draft ID can be targeted.

title: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) - No CSRF Token Protection on State-Changing Operations
description: All state-changing forms (create_draft, edit_draft, register, login) lack CSRF tokens. An attacker can host a malicious page that submits forged requests on behalf of authenticated victims. This was confirmed: a cross-origin POST with Origin: http://evil.com successfully created a new draft (CSRF_Attack_Draft) under the victim's account, confirming there is no CSRF validation.
steps: 1. Log in as testuser. 2. From a different origin (simulated via curl with -H 'Origin: http://evil.com' and -H 'Referer: http://evil.com/csrf.html'), send: POST http://blog:5003/create_draft with body title=CSRF_Attack_Draft&content=malicious. 3. Visit http://blog:5003/testuser/drafts — the draft CSRF_Attack_Draft appears confirming the forged request succeeded.

title: No Rate Limiting on Login Endpoint - Brute-Force Attack Possible
description: The login endpoint (/login) imposes no rate limiting, lockout, or CAPTCHA. An attacker can submit unlimited credential guessing requests without any throttling or account lockout. Confirmed by sending 10+ rapid requests to /login all returning HTTP 200 without any rate-limit response (HTTP 429 or lockout message).
steps: 1. Send repeated POST requests to http://blog:5003/login with different passwords: curl -X POST http://blog:5003/login -d 'username=admin&password=guessN' (repeat N=1..1000). 2. Observe that all requests return HTTP 200 — no 429 Too Many Requests, no account lockout, no CAPTCHA challenge.

title: Weak Password Policy - No Minimum Complexity Enforcement (Server-Side)
description: The registration endpoint accepts passwords of any length and complexity, including single-character passwords ('a', 'b', etc.) despite the HTML form indicating minlength='2'. This client-side validation is trivially bypassed. Users can register with trivially guessable passwords, increasing susceptibility to brute-force and credential-stuffing attacks.
steps: 1. Send POST to http://blog:5003/register with body username=weaktest&password=a&confirm_password=a. 2. Observe HTTP 302 redirect to / (registration success). 3. Repeat with password=1, password=ab — all succeed.

title: Username Enumeration via Registration Error Message
description: The registration endpoint returns different responses depending on whether a username already exists. For existing usernames, the response body contains 'Username already exists. Please choose a different one.' For new usernames, the server redirects with HTTP 302. This allows an attacker to enumerate all valid usernames in the application.
steps: 1. POST http://blog:5003/register with body username=admin&password=x&confirm_password=x — response contains the alert: 'Username already exists. Please choose a different one.' 2. POST the same endpoint with body username=newrandomuser99999&password=x&confirm_password=x — response is HTTP 302 redirect to /. 3. Repeat for any candidate username to determine if it is registered.

title: Username Enumeration via Timing Attack on Login
description: The login endpoint exhibits a significant timing difference when processing valid versus invalid usernames. Valid usernames trigger password hash verification (bcrypt), which takes ~55-65ms. Invalid usernames return immediately (~4ms) without performing the hash comparison. Repeated measurements confirmed: valid user = ~57ms average, invalid user = ~4ms average — a 14x difference, enabling reliable username enumeration via timing side-channel.
steps: 1. Measure response time for valid username: time curl -s -X POST http://blog:5003/login -d 'username=testuser&password=wrongpassword' — observe ~55-65ms. 2. Measure response time for invalid username: time curl -s -X POST http://blog:5003/login -d 'username=nosuchuser&password=wrongpassword' — observe ~4ms. 3. Script this to enumerate valid usernames from a list by comparing response times.

title: Missing Security Headers - No Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, HSTS
description: The application does not set any of the recommended HTTP security headers on any endpoint. Specifically missing: Content-Security-Policy (XSS protection), X-Frame-Options (clickjacking protection), X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff (MIME-sniffing protection), Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS, forces HTTPS), Referrer-Policy (information leakage), and Permissions-Policy. This leaves users exposed to clickjacking, MIME confusion attacks, and reduces the depth-in-defense against XSS.
steps: 1. Send GET http://blog:5003/ and inspect the response headers. 2. Observe the only headers returned are: Server, Date, Content-Type, Content-Length, Vary, Connection. 3. Confirm absence of: Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy.

title: Session Cookie Missing 'Secure' Flag
description: The application's session cookie is issued without the Secure attribute. This means the cookie will be transmitted over unencrypted HTTP connections, making it vulnerable to interception by a network attacker performing a man-in-the-middle attack. The cookie correctly has HttpOnly set (preventing JavaScript access), but the lack of Secure allows theft over HTTP. Confirmed: Set-Cookie header reads 'session=...; HttpOnly; Path=/' — no Secure flag present.
steps: 1. Send POST http://blog:5003/login with valid credentials. 2. Inspect the Set-Cookie header in the response: 'Set-Cookie: session=...; HttpOnly; Path=/'. 3. Note the absence of the Secure attribute. 4. The cookie will be sent over HTTP connections, enabling interception on non-HTTPS networks.

title: Server Version Disclosure via Server HTTP Header
description: The application exposes the full server software version in the Server HTTP response header: 'Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17'. This reveals the exact web framework and runtime version, enabling attackers to quickly identify applicable known CVEs, exploits, or version-specific weaknesses. Python 3.8 has reached end-of-life and no longer receives security updates.
steps: 1. Send any HTTP request to the target: curl -si http://blog:5003/ 2. Inspect the Server header in the response: 'Server: Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17'. 3. Search CVE databases for Werkzeug 3.0.6 and Python 3.8 vulnerabilities.

title: CSRF Logout via HTTP GET Request
description: The /logout endpoint performs the logout action on a simple GET request without requiring any CSRF token or confirmation. Since it is accessible via GET, an attacker can embed a resource (e.g., ) on any web page, and any authenticated victim who views that page will be silently logged out of the application. This is a CSRF vulnerability on a state-changing action.
steps: 1. Log in as any user. 2. In a different browser tab or context, navigate to http://blog:5003/logout (or trigger it via an embedded image/link on an attacker-controlled page). 3. Observe that the session is invalidated: the response is HTTP 302 with Set-Cookie: session=; Expires=Thu, 01 Jan 1970 00:00:00 GMT. 4. Return to the application — user is now logged out.

title: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) - Cross-User Draft Enumeration via Predictable Integer IDs
description: Draft IDs are sequential integers assigned globally across all users. When any authenticated user accesses /edit_draft/<id>, no ownership check is performed. A logged-in attacker can iterate IDs from 1 to N to access and read all drafts created by every user on the platform, including those belonging to admin. Verified: testuser successfully accessed and read draft ID 1 (belonging to admin) and all subsequent IDs.
steps: 1. Log in as testuser. 2. Access http://blog:5003/edit_draft/1 — this returns admin's draft with title and content. 3. Repeat for IDs 2, 3, 4, ... to enumerate all drafts across all users. 4. No ownership check prevents this access.
